summary: The Dashboard Approval Backend (F002-BE) is well-implemented. The service correctly aggregates pending approvals across Kas, Bank, and Gudang modules, routes approve/reject actions to the correct module handlers, enforces business rules (creator cannot approve own transaction, different approvers for stage 1 and 2 via existing module functions), and adds useful utilities like overdue calculation. All validators passed (typecheck, lint, 145 tests including 20 new dashboard approval tests). Minor code-quality observations noted around SQL injection surface and type declarations, but nothing blocking.

issues.description: String-interpolated SQL status values in getPendingFromModule, getApprovalCounts, and getPendingApprovals queries create a small SQL injection surface even though the injected strings come from internal TRANSACTION_STATUS constants. Best practice is to use parameterized queries or the sql.js parameter binding mechanism to align with project conventions.

IPC handlers were added to main.ts and preload.ts, but electron.d.ts was not updated with the new Dashboard Approval API signatures, which means renderer TypeScript consumers may see implicit-any usage until the declarations are added.

The dashboardApproval tests mock the sql.js default export but never exercise getPendingApprovals or getApprovalCounts against an actual in-memory SQL.js database, leaving the cross-period aggregation and filtering paths lightly verified. Existing coverage is via routing/permission tests only.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The backend-worker SKILL.md documents updating electron.d.ts as a required IPC step, but the worker did not do this for F002-BE. The skill example shows it explicitly under 'Implement IPC Handlers' step 5. This suggests the skill's checklist step should be highlighted more prominently or tracked in handoff verification.

AGENTS.md states 'Always use parameterized queries' under Database conventions. The dashboardApproval service interpolates status strings into SQL (e.g., WHERE t.status IN ('${pendingStatus1}', '${pendingStatus2}')), which technically deviates from the convention even though the values are internal constants. A documented example of safe sql.js pattern for IN-clauses with internal constants could close this gap.
services.yaml already includes the necessary test/lint/typecheck commands, and the worker used them correctly. No services gap was observed.
